narrative: I'm sorry to share that your law school application was rejected because our predictive model estimated a 46% chance that you might fail the bar exam on your first try, which is above our threshold for approval. This prediction is based on your academic history, including your undergraduate GPA of 2.10—well below the typical 3.21 average—and your LSAT score of 34, compared to the usual 36.7. Your school decile rankings also played a key role: you ranked in the 3rd decile for Year 1 grades (versus the average 5.71) and 2nd decile for Year 3 (against 5.53), signaling performance lower than most peers. Your upper-middle family income bracket aligns closely with the dataset average, while being male and White matches the most common profiles (56% male, 84% White), and planning full-time study is typical for 93% of students. Among these, your Year 3 decile ranking had the strongest influence in pushing the model toward predicting failure, followed closely by your Year 1 decile, then your LSAT score. Next in line were your undergraduate GPA and family income, with your racial background, gender, and full-time status having smaller but still contributing effects—all nudging the prediction toward a higher failure risk compared to stronger applicants. Typical admitted students have higher deciles, GPAs around 3.2 or above, and LSATs in the mid-to-upper 30s, putting you below those benchmarks across the board. While demographics like gender and race didn't sway things much in your case, your academic metrics stood out as the main drivers. The good news is you can take action: focus on boosting your academic deciles through stronger coursework or targeted study habits if reapplying after more school years. Retaking the LSAT to aim above 36 could make a big difference, as it's a changeable factor with high influence. Building a higher undergraduate-equivalent record, perhaps through post-bac courses, might also help offset your current GPA. With these improvements, your profile could shift toward the passing prediction territory we see in successful applicants—keep pushing, and consider bar prep resources early to build confidence.
